my_sentence: I had some free time to learn new knowledge.
correction: I had some free time to learn some new vocabulary words.
explanation: In English you 'learn vocabulary words' (or just 'new words'); 'learn knowledge' is not a natural collocation — you 'gain' or 'acquire' knowledge.
correct_examples: I spent the evening learning new vocabulary words.
She acquired a lot of knowledge from the course.
context: How Jack spent his day

my_sentence: What of the other?
correction: What about the others?
explanation: To ask for the next point, use the fixed phrase 'What about ...?', not 'What of ...?'. Since you mean the rest of the people, use the plural pronoun 'the others'.
correct_examples: That's one option. What about the others?
What about the other team members?
context: Discussing people who praise vs. people who complain

my_sentence: When we find some problems, it bothers me, it bothers also.
correction: When we find problems, it will bother us.
explanation: You were speaking generally about everyone, so the object should be 'us', not 'me'. Use 'will bother' for the expected result.
correct_examples: When the system fails, it will bother us all.
These bugs bother us every day.
context: Why people complain about problems

my_sentence: They will complain our system.
correction: They will complain about our system.
explanation: 'complain' needs the preposition 'about' before the thing you're unhappy with: 'complain about something'.
correct_examples: Customers complain about the long wait.
He always complains about the weather.
context: Customers complaining about the store system

my_sentence: Cost, quality and deliver time.
correction: Cost, quality and delivery time.
explanation: Before a noun, use the noun form 'delivery' ('delivery time'), not the verb 'deliver'.
correct_examples: We compare price, quality and delivery time.
Fast delivery time is a selling point.
context: Different perspectives for judging a product

my_sentence: If you make a mistake, you need to apology for it.
correction: If you make a mistake, you need to apologize for it.
explanation: 'apology' is the noun; the verb is 'apologize'. After 'need to' you need the verb: 'apologize for it'.
correct_examples: You should apologize for being late.
He apologized for the mistake.
context: What to do after making a mistake at work

my_sentence: They don't want to talk with you.
correction: They don't want to talk to you.
explanation: Here the natural preposition with 'talk' is 'to' — 'talk to someone'. ('talk with' exists, but the teacher preferred 'talk to you'.)
correct_examples: I need to talk to you for a minute.
Nobody wanted to talk to him after that.
context: How a bad attitude affects coworkers

my_sentence: You need to ask other to join.
correction: You need to ask others to join you.
explanation: Use the plural 'others' for other people, and add the object 'you' — they join you.
correct_examples: You can ask others to join you on the project.
She asked the others to help her.
context: You can't finish a project alone

my_sentence: They will preach / prize / pray about it.
correction: They will praise it.
explanation: 'praise' (/preɪz/) kept coming out as 'pray', 'prize' or 'preach'. Keep the long /eɪ/ followed by a /z/ sound, distinct from those other words.
correct_examples: The manager will praise good work.
She praised the team for their effort.
context: People who find things to praise

my_sentence: I go there one time a year, or one time in two years.
correction: I go there once a year, or once in every two years.
explanation: For frequency, use 'once' (one time) and 'twice' (two times) instead of 'one time' / 'two times'. For every second year, say 'once in every two years'.
correct_examples: I see the dentist twice a year.
We meet once in every two years.
context: How often Jack visits the dental clinic

my_sentence: Taiwan only has 24 millions people.
correction: Taiwan only has about 25 million people.
explanation: After an exact number, 'million' stays singular: '25 million people'. Only use 'millions of' (with -s) when there is no specific number.
correct_examples: The city has three million residents.
Millions of people watched the game.
context: Comparing the populations of China and Taiwan

my_sentence: I don't have pretty good communicating skills.
correction: I don't have pretty good communication skills.
explanation: The fixed compound noun is 'communication skills' — singular 'communication' plus plural 'skills'. Not 'communicating' or 'communications'.
correct_examples: Good communication skills are essential.
The job requires strong communication skills.
context: Talking about Jack's communication ability

my_sentence: For me, I don't have pretty good communication skills.
correction: My communication skills aren't pretty good.
explanation: It's more natural to make 'my communication skills' the subject than to say 'I don't have good ...'. Also drop the redundant 'For me, I'.
correct_examples: My writing skills aren't very strong yet.
His English isn't very good.
context: Assessing his own communication skills

my_sentence: I can't understand others mining.
correction: I can't understand what others mean.
explanation: Use the noun clause 'what others mean' as the object of 'understand'. ('mean' is the verb here; 'mining' was a slip.)
correct_examples: I can't understand what they mean.
Do you understand what I mean?
context: Difficulty in communication

my_sentence: I need to prepare many times.
correction: I need to prepare for it many times.
explanation: When there's an object, 'prepare' takes 'for': 'prepare for it/something'.
correct_examples: I need to prepare for the meeting.
They prepared for the exam all week.
context: Why Jack can't give a speech without a script

my_sentence: The result is not certified.
correction: The result is not satisfying.
explanation: To say a result isn't good enough, use 'satisfying' (or 'satisfactory'). 'certified' means officially approved — a different meaning.
correct_examples: The outcome wasn't satisfying.
Her answer was satisfactory.
context: Why an unprepared speech turns out badly

my_sentence: We more care about the result.
correction: We care more about the result.
explanation: The adverb 'more' comes after the verb 'care': 'care more about ...', not 'more care about'. The teacher corrected this several times today.
correct_examples: We care more about quality than speed.
I care more about the team than the title.
context: What modern tech companies value

my_sentence: Like assembly line worker, security guard and nanny.
correction: Like assembly-line workers, security guards and nannies.
explanation: When talking about a type of job in general, use the plural: 'workers', 'security guards', 'nannies'.
correct_examples: Jobs like nurses and teachers help people.
Security guards work long shifts.
context: Jobs where 'arrive early, work late' applies

my_sentence: If they always late and forget something, you will let them go.
correction: If they are always late and forget things, you will let them go.
explanation: 'late' is an adjective, so it needs the verb 'be': 'they are always late'. Don't drop the 'are'.
correct_examples: If he is always late, he'll be fired.
They are always on time.
context: Why nannies should not arrive late

my_sentence: They need to dress in a business-like way to show their professional and respect.
correction: They need to dress in a business-like way to show their professionalism and respect.
explanation: 'professional' is an adjective; here, after 'their' and joined with the noun 'respect', you need the noun 'professionalism'.
correct_examples: A suit shows your professionalism.
They valued his professionalism and honesty.
context: Why doctors and business people dress formally

my_sentence: I will learn new skills to improve my effective.
correction: I will learn new skills to improve my effectiveness. / ...to make my projects more effective.
explanation: 'effective' is an adjective, so it can't follow 'my'. Use the noun 'effectiveness' ('my effectiveness'), or keep the adjective before a noun ('effective projects'). Forms: effect (noun), effective (adjective), effectively (adverb).
correct_examples: Training improved my effectiveness at work.
The new tool makes the team more effective.
context: How learning skills makes you valuable

my_sentence: So we need to make ourselves valuable. So it is very important to improve our skills.
correction: We need to make ourselves valuable, so it is very important to improve our skills.
explanation: 'so' is a conjunction that joins clauses, so avoid starting a sentence with it. Either join the two clauses with 'so', or begin the new sentence with a different word. The teacher flagged this repeatedly today.
correct_examples: The deadline is close, so we must hurry.
It was raining, so we stayed inside.
context: Making yourself valuable so you won't be fired

my_sentence: First it can help the client. Second it can improve professionalism.
correction: First, it can help the client. Secondly, it can improve professionalism.
explanation: When listing points, use the ordinal adverbs 'First(ly), Secondly, Thirdly' (followed by a comma), not the bare adjective 'Second'.
correct_examples: Firstly, it saves money. Secondly, it saves time.
Secondly, we should test the code.
context: Listing the advantages of dressing formally

my_sentence: If others don't know about it, you can't get promotion.
correction: If others don't know about it, you can't get promoted.
explanation: Say 'get promoted' (passive verb) or 'get a promotion' (with the article 'a'). 'get promotion' without an article isn't natural.
correct_examples: He worked hard and got promoted.
She's hoping to get a promotion this year.
context: Why visibility matters for keeping your job
